examples.title: NFA to DFA Conversion Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm to convert Non-deterministic Finite Automaton to Equivalent Deterministic Finite Automaton using Subset Construction
examples.code:
ALGORITHM NFA_to_DFA
INPUT: NFA = (Q, Σ, δ, q0, F)
OUTPUT: DFA = (Q', Σ, δ', q0', F')

BEGIN
 // Step 1: Find ε-closure for all states
 FOR each state q IN Q DO
 ε_closure[q] := ComputeEpsilonClosure(q)
 END FOR
 
 // Step 2: Initialize DFA
 Q' := {} // Set of DFA states
 q0' := ε_closure[q0] // Start state
 ADD q0' TO Q'
 F' := {} // Accepting states
 
 // Step 3: Process new states
 WHILE new_state IN Q' UNPROCESSED DO
 FOR each input symbol a IN Σ DO
 // Find all states reachable from current NFA states
 // on input 'a' followed by ε-closure
 new_set := {}
 FOR each state s IN current_NFA_states DO
 FOR each state t IN δ(s, a) DO
 ADD ε_closure[t] TO new_set
 END FOR
 END FOR
 
 // Add transition in DFA
 δ'(current_NFA_states, a) := new_set
 
 // If new state not in Q', add it
 IF new_set NOT IN Q' THEN
 ADD new_set TO Q'
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 // Mark current state as processed
 MARK current_NFA_states AS PROCESSED
 
 // Check if this state contains any NFA accepting state
 IF new_set ∩ F ≠ ∅ THEN
 ADD new_set TO F'
 END IF
 END WHILE
 
 RETURN DFA
END
examples.explanation: The algorithm uses subset construction where each DFA state represents a set of NFA states. The ε-closure ensures all states reachable via ε-transitions are included. The process continues until no new states are generated.

examples.title: Thompson's Construction Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm to convert Regular Expression to NFA using recursive construction
examples.explanation: Thompson's Construction builds NFAs recursively. For each operation (union, concatenation, star), it combines smaller NFAs. The algorithm guarantees that the resulting NFA has at most 2|RE| states and can be simulated efficiently.

examples.title: DFA Minimization Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm to minimize a DFA by merging equivalent states
examples.code:
ALGORITHM MinimizeDFA(DFA)
INPUT: DFA = (Q, Σ, δ, q0, F)
OUTPUT: Minimal DFA

BEGIN
 // Step 1: Initial Partition
 // Group states into accepting and non-accepting
 P := {F, Q - F} // Two groups
 
 REPEAT
 New_P := P
 FOR each group G IN P DO
 // Try to split group into subgroups
 FOR each state pair (p, q) IN G DO
 // Check if p and q are distinguishable
 FOR each input symbol a IN Σ DO
 δ(p, a) = p', δ(q, a) = q'
 // Find groups containing p' and q'
 group_p := FindGroup(P, p')
 group_q := FindGroup(P, q')
 
 IF group_p ≠ group_q THEN
 // p and q are distinguishable
 Split G into G1 (with p) and G2 (with q)
 Replace G with G1, G2 in New_P
 BREAK
 END IF
 END FOR
 END FOR
 END FOR
 P := New_P
 UNTIL P = New_P // No more splitting
 
 // Step 2: Construct Minimal DFA
 // Each group becomes one state in minimal DFA
 FOR each group G IN P DO
 new_state := representative of G
 // Transitions: for each input a
 δ'(new_state, a) := representative of group containing δ(rep, a)
 END FOR
 
 q0' := group containing q0
 F' := groups that contain any accepting state
 
 RETURN Minimal DFA
END
examples.explanation: The algorithm uses partition refinement. Initially, states are divided into accepting and non-accepting. Then iteratively, groups are split if states within a group behave differently on some input. States in the same final group are equivalent and can be merged.
